hero_text: OpenAI just released a new model that might actually be useful for your job — and we'll tell you exactly what that means. Also: the word 'hallucination' finally makes sense, a free tool that summarizes any document in seconds, and an organization teaching seniors to use AI so they can live independently longer.
opening_headline:
Everyone is talking about AI.
Here is what they actually mean.
opening_body:
My mother called me last week to ask if she needed to "learn the AI" before her job made her. She is a school librarian. She has been a school librarian for twenty-six years. She is very good at her job. She should not have to spend her weekends anxious about a technology that most people in tech barely understand themselves.

That call is why this newsletter exists. Not for people who already know what a large language model is. For my mother. For the teacher who got an email from the principal about "AI integration" and has no idea what that means in practice. For the person who has heard the word ChatGPT so many times they're embarrassed to admit they've never actually tried it.

AI is genuinely interesting and genuinely useful and genuinely confusing, and there is almost nothing written about it for people who aren't already deep inside it. Robots Think is the attempt to fix that. We explain what's happening, we tell you what to actually do about it, and we do both of those things without making you feel like you should already know.

Welcome to Issue No. 1. Let's get into it.
hear_headline:
What happened in AI
this week, translated.

hear_items.headline: OpenAI Released a New Model — Here Is What That Actually Means
hear_items.body: OpenAI, the company behind ChatGPT, released an updated version of its AI model this week. A "model" is basically the brain inside the AI — the part that reads your question and figures out what to say. The new version is faster and better at following instructions precisely, which means it makes fewer strange mistakes.

hear_items.headline: The EU's AI Rules Are Now Actually in Effect for the Biggest Systems
hear_items.body: The European Union passed a law called the AI Act, and the first batch of rules took effect this month. The short version: companies must be more transparent about how their AI works. This mostly affects the companies building AI, not you, but it's the first major law of its kind anywhere in the world.

hear_items.headline: Google Added AI Summaries to Search — and People Have Feelings
hear_items.body: Google now shows an AI-generated summary at the top of many search results before you see any actual links. Some people love it, some people hate it (the AI sometimes gets things wrong). You can turn it off in Google's settings if you prefer the old way.

know_headline:
When AI makes something up,
there is a word for that.
know_intro: You may have heard that AI chatbots sometimes "hallucinate." It is a strange word for a straightforward problem: AI systems sometimes confidently state things that are completely false. A fake court case. A book that doesn't exist. The AI doesn't know it's wrong — it's not lying, exactly — it's more like a student who didn't study but writes an essay anyway and fills in the gaps with plausible-sounding nonsense.
know_callout: The technical reason this happens: AI models like ChatGPT are trained to predict the most likely next word in a sentence, based on patterns in enormous amounts of text. They are very good at sounding right. They are not always good at being right. These are related but different skills.
know_body:
This matters practically because AI is genuinely useful for drafting things, summarizing things, and brainstorming — but it should not be the last word on anything factual without you checking. Think of it like a very fast, very confident research assistant who occasionally invents sources.

The good news: newer AI systems are getting better at citing actual sources you can verify, and at saying "I don't know" when they don't. Always double-check anything important before you act on it.
know_quote: "AI systems are extraordinarily good at fluency and extraordinarily unreliable as a source of ground truth. They are not search engines. They are pattern-completion engines." — Arvind Narayanan, Professor of Computer Science, Princeton University
see_headline:
There is a free tool
that summarizes any document
in about thirty seconds.
see_body:
Claude.ai (the AI made by Anthropic) has a free version that is genuinely excellent at one specific thing: reading a long document and telling you what's in it. You paste in text — or upload a PDF — and ask it to summarize. The summary is usually clear and accurate, and you can ask follow-up questions like "what does paragraph 4 mean in plain English?"

The one thing to try: find something you've been meaning to read but haven't — a lease, a report, a long article — and paste it into claude.ai with the message "summarize this in plain language and flag anything I should pay attention to." It takes less than a minute.
see_image_caption: Claude.ai · Free AI assistant from Anthropic
help_intro: Older Adults Technology Services (OATS) has been teaching digital skills to seniors since 2004. Now they've expanded their curriculum to include AI tools — because they've seen how these tools can help older adults live independently longer and navigate systems that increasingly assume everyone is comfortable with technology.
help_stat_label: older adults trained in digital skills since founding
help_body: A 79-year-old participant named Dorothy described learning to use an AI assistant to draft messages to her doctor as "the first time technology felt like it was actually on my side." OATS runs free programs in community centers and libraries across the country. What they need most right now is funding to expand their AI-specific curriculum to more cities.
tears_headline:
He had 21 minutes.
The crowd had 72,000 people.
He ran every single one of them.
tears_body:
It was July 13, 1985, and Freddie Mercury had been told to cut it short. The Live Aid set was scheduled, staged, managed down to the minute — a global broadcast feeding 1.9 billion people across 150 countries. Every artist was on the clock.

Freddie Mercury was not brief. He walked out and proceeded to run Wembley Stadium — all 72,000 people in it — for 21 minutes in a way that music historians still struggle to fully account for. At one point he simply leaned into the microphone, made a sound, and 72,000 people made it back.

David Bowie was there. Paul McCartney was there. Freddie Mercury finished his set, waved once, walked off, and everyone understood they had just watched something that wouldn't happen again. They were right.
